summary: Gruber analyzes a New York Times report on internal criticism of Google CEO Sundar Pichai, arguing that the article's dramatic framing overstates the actual problem. He contends that Pichai has simply made Google more focused and disciplined, which some executives experience as risk aversion. Gruber draws a comparison to Apple's famous 'thousand no's for every yes' philosophy, noting that focus and saying no have been central to Apple's success since Steve Jobs's return in 1997. He acknowledges Google's strong financial results under Pichai while noting that the disgruntled executives preferred the old Google culture of pursuing clever ideas regardless of strategic value.
key_points: Wakabayashi's NYT lede is more dramatic than the substance warrants — the real story is that Pichai made Google more focused and boring
The internal critics preferred Google's old culture of pursuing ideas because they seemed cool, not because they were strategically sound (e.g., Google Glass)
Apple's 'thousand no's for every yes' philosophy and Jobs's 'focusing is about saying no' provide a direct parallel to what Pichai is doing at Google
Google's financial results under Pichai have been strong, undermining the case that his approach is failing
The rejected Shopify acquisition is framed as overcaution by critics, but could equally be read as disciplined focus
money_quotes: I'd argue that it boils down to the fact that Pichai has transformed Google into a more focused, and perhaps more boring, company, and that his internal critics preferred the old Google culture — one that did things just because they seemed clever or cool, not because they were necessarily strategically useful to the company.
It seems undeniable that under Pichai, Google is more focused: more no's, fewer yes's. The sources in Wakabayashi's report clearly want more yes's.
One person's overcaution is another's focus.
A comparison to Apple (shocking, coming from me, I know) is apt.
But that 'thousand no's for every yes' mantra wasn't defining a new Apple, it was clarifying that post-Jobs Apple would remain the same Apple.
key_insight: What Google insiders frame as Pichai's risk aversion and overcaution is equally readable as the kind of strategic focus that has long been central to Apple's success.
